segments.text: Welcome to Planetarian Daily. I'm Patrick and Vancouver, and this is Episode 37. It's March 29th,
2026. Today, we're diving into a collection of studies that each, in their own way, pull back
the curtain on systems we thought we understood reasonably well, only to discover there's still
remarkable depth left to explore. Let's begin inside the cell itself, at a scale that's almost
impossible to visualize. Researchers at the Italian Institute of Technology, working with colleagues
at Uppsala University and AstraZeneca have built and run what might be the most ambitious molecular
simulation of the spliceosum to date. Using advanced computational chemistry and serious super
computing power, they constructed a model containing two million atoms that tries to represent a realistic
slice of a human cell. Inside that model, they let the spliceosum run through its full range of motions.
The spliceosum is the massive molecular machine responsible for RNA splicing,
cutting out introns and stitching exons back together so that a single gene can produce multiple
protein variants. Until now, most of what we knew came from static snapshots taken by cryo-electron
microscopy. Those images are incredibly valuable, but they're frozen moments. This new work gives us
something closer to a movie. You can watch the machines moving parts coordinate in three dimensions,
see how it grips the RNA, how it bends and repositions itself, and how different subunits talk to one
another during the catalytic cycle. What stands out to me is how this bridges two worlds that have
historically been quite separate structural biology and simulation science. The level of detail now
available means we can start asking questions that were previously theoretical. Why do certain mutations
stall the machine at specific points? How do small molecules change the choreography? Could we design
drugs that nudge the spliceosum back into correct behavior in diseases where splicing goes wrong?
Because faulty splicing is implicated in a range of conditions from some forms of spinal muscular
atrophy to quite a few cancers, having a dynamic model like this feels like gaining a new set of
eyes. The obvious next chapter is whether these simulations can be scaled and made fast enough to
test libraries of compounds or patient specific mutations in something approaching real-time.
If that becomes practical, we could see a noticeable acceleration in how splicing targeted therapies
move from concept to clinic. It's a beautiful example of computation letting us observe biology that
simply too fast and too small for any microscope we currently have. While that simulation lets us watch
one of the cell's most intricate machines in motion, another large-scale effort has given us clarity
on an entirely different set of proteins that touch almost every aspect of cellular life.
A global collaboration led by researchers at WEHI in Australia has produced the first
comprehensive authoritative atlas of all human E3 ubiquitin legacies. These enzymes are the cells
master regulators of protein degradation and signaling. They decide which proteins get tagged for the
proteosum, which get modified to change their function, and they play central roles in everything
from cell cycle control to DNA repair and immune signaling. For nearly two decades, the field has been
working with inconsistent sometimes contradictory data sets about how many E3 legacies there actually are,
what their true substrates are and how specific they really are. The new atlas published in cell
sets a new gold standard. It brings together structural, biochemical, and functional data into one
coherent reference that should reduce wasted effort and misdirected hypotheses across hundreds of lab.
I find this story quietly exciting because E3 legacies have become extremely attractive drug targets
in recent years. The development of protax, those bifunctional molecules that hijack in E3 to
degrade a disease-causing protein, depends on knowing exactly which legacies are available and how
they behave. A reliable map like this doesn't just tidy up the literature. It removes a layer of friction
that has slowed progress. It's the kind of foundational resource that tends to pay dividends for
a decade or more. Staying with fundamental biology but shifting upward in scale, let's move from
the machinery inside individual cells to the machinery inside our own heads. A paper in physical
review letters takes a careful second look at the idea that the human brain operates exactly at a
critical point. The sweet spot between order and chaos that some theorists have argued gives brains
the remarkable balance of stability and flexibility using whole brain FMRI data and a more statistically
robust analytical framework than earlier studies. The researchers show that many of the signatures
people have interpreted as evidence of criticality are actually statistical artifacts. The brain does
appear to function near a critical point but not precisely at it. There's a meaningful distinction
here. Operating right at criticality would make the system exquisitely sensitive to small inputs but
also potentially unstable. Sitting slightly off that edge might give the brain just enough margin
to remain robust while still retaining the advantages of operating close to the transition.
This refines rather than overturns the criticality hypothesis and that's exactly the kind of
incremental clarification I appreciate. It pushes modelers to be more precise about what near critical
actually means for information processing learning and adaptability. For those of us who follow neuroscience,
it's another reminder that the brain is subtle. The elegant theories sometimes need tempering by
rigorous data analysis. From the delicate balance inside the brain, we shift to an even older
partnership. One that's been shaping both species for far longer than most of us learned in school.
An analysis of ancient DNA published in Nature reveals that dogs were already widely distributed across
Western Eurasia during the Paleolithic, living and working alongside humans much earlier than
previously thought. The study significantly extends the timeline of one of humanity's oldest interspecies
relationships. We're not just talking about a few isolated finds. The genetic evidence points to
dogs being part of human groups across a broad geographic area at a time when we were still hunter
gatherers. This deeper history changes how we think about the process of domestication.
It suggests the relationship may have begun more gradually and in more places than the classic single
origin stories implied. What I find moving about this work is how it underscores the reciprocity.
Dogs didn't just adapt to us, we adapted to them. Their presence likely improved our hunting
success, our safety, even our social lives. In return, we provided them with reliable food and protection.
The partnership has been mutual for so long that it's become part of who both species are.
Every time we see a new ancient DNA paper like this, it feels like we're slowly filling in the early
chapters of a shared story that's still being written. While ancient DNA is illuminating our past
on earth, new observations are revealing hidden activity deep inside our own star. Researchers at NYU
Abu Dhabi have detected large-scale magnetic waves moving through regions of the sun that have remained
largely inaccessible to direct observation. These waves are driven by magnetic fields far below the
surface, and they appear to propagate in ways that give us a new window into the sun's interior
dynamics. Until recently, much of what we knew about the sun's deep layers came from theoretical
models and indirect measurements of surface oscillations. These newly observed magnetic waves act
almost like a seismic probe, carrying information about temperature, density, and magnetic field strength
from regions we couldn't easily reach before. The hope is that this data will improve our ability to
model the solar dynamo, the process that generates the sun's magnetic field, and drives the roughly
11-year cycle of sunspots, flares, and coronal mass ejections. Better long-term forecasts of
solar activity matter for satellites, power grids, aviation, and communications. Space weather isn't
just an academic curiosity, it's an infrastructure issue, so any new tool that lets us see deeper into
the sun's engine room deserves attention. It's early days, but the detection itself is the kind of
observational advance that often opens an entire new chapter in solar physics. Speaking of
energy systems, two quite different stories this week look at how we might produce cleaner energy
and materials. In the first, researchers demonstrated singlet fission in a solar cell architecture using a
spin-flip metal complex. The system is able to produce more energy carriers than the number of photons
absorbed, reaching approximately 130% efficiency in terms of excitons generated. Singlet fission is
a quantum process in which a single high energy photon can generate two lower energy excitons
instead of just one. It's one of the more promising routes around the shock-ly-quicer limit that has
capped conventional solar cell efficiency for decades. What makes this particular demonstration notable
is that they achieved it with a relatively stable metal complex approach rather than purely organic
materials that have sometimes proven fragile. It's still laboratory scale, but it adds to the growing
sense that third-generation photovoltaic concepts are moving from theoretical curiosity toward
something that could eventually appear in real panels. While that work is about capturing more energy
from the Sun, another group has found a smarter way to capture something we urgently need to remove
from the atmosphere. Scientists have developed a nitrogen-tuned carbon material that captures carbon
dioxide more efficiently and crucially releases the captured CO2 at much lower temperatures,
sometimes below 60 degrees Celsius. That means the regeneration step could potentially run on low
grade waste heat rather than requiring high-energy steam or other intensive inputs.
Traditional amine-based carbon capture systems are effective but energy-hungry, which is one reason
they've been slow to scale. Lowering the temperature needed to release the CO2 changes the energy
equation in a meaningful way and could bring the overall cost down substantially. The material itself
is built on carbon, which is abundant and relatively inexpensive to produce at scale.
It's another incremental but practical step toward making carbon capture something that industries
might actually choose to deploy rather than simply comply with. That's one approach to dealing
with carbon. In a very different direction, researchers at Xi'an-Jiao-Tong University have used
improved AI models to systematically identify entirely new solid forms of carbon. Some of the
predicted structures are expected to be harder than diamond and show exotic combinations of
properties that don't exist in graphite diamond or the better-known carbon allotropes. What I like
about this story is the methodological advance. Rather than relying on serendipity or exhaustive
trial and error in the lab, they've trained models that can explore vast regions of structural space
that would be impractical to test experimentally. The AI essentially acts as a hypothesis generator,
suggesting candidates that are then evaluated with more computationally expensive methods.
This approach is already being applied to other materials classes and it feels like the beginning
of a broader shift in how we discover new substances. The potential applications range from cutting tools
to electronics to quantum technologies. Pretty much anywhere extreme hardness or unusual electronic
properties would be useful. From new materials to rethinking how we make existing ones, chemists at
Wageningen University and research in Utrecht University have developed an electrochemical method
to produce a key chemical building block directly from biomass. The precursor in question is used in
plastics, pharmaceuticals and fragrances, and the traditional production routes often rely on
hazardous reagents and harsh conditions. Their electrochemical approach works in a relatively
simple setup avoids those dangerous chemicals and uses renewable feedstock. Green chemistry advances
like this don't always make headlines but they matter. Every time we can replace a toxic feedstock or a
high-energy process with something milder and cleaner, we reduce both environmental burden and safety
risks in manufacturing. It's also a nice demonstration of how electrochemistry powered increasingly
by renewable electricity can serve as a bridge between biomass and the chemicals we still need in
everyday products. While those stories focus on potential solutions, two environmental studies this
week highlight the law of unintended consequences that often accompanies our clean energy push.
A team at Helmholtz Zentrum here on examined how offshore wind farms are altering natural sediment
transport in the North Sea, with the German bite particularly affected. The structures appear to be
changing current patterns and could move up to 1.5 million tons of sediment annually in ways that
weren't fully anticipated. We tend to think of wind farms as almost purely beneficial, clean electricity
with no direct emissions. This research is a useful reminder that large physical structures and
dynamic marine environments will always have secondary effects. Coastal ecosystems, sediment balances
and even the long-term stability of the seabed around the turbines deserve careful monitoring as
offshore wind capacity continues to grow. The goal isn't to stop deployment but to deploy with
eyes open. A broader analysis led by German Bersali and colleagues and published in current research
and environmental sustainability looked at four European countries and asked a tougher question.
Are we actually achieving the deep systemic change we claim? Or are we mostly substituting one
energy source for another while leaving the underlying structure of our economies and energy systems
largely intact? Their method suggests that none of the studied nations has yet completed the full
structural transformation required for genuine sustainability. Emissions cuts and rising renewable
shares are necessary but not sufficient. The paper encourages us to look beyond headline numbers at
things like energy return on investment, material flows, land use change, and the persistence of
certain high impact industries. It's the kind of sobering systems level thinking that's easy to
dismiss is overly pessimistic but important to sit with. Real progress requires examining the
entire system, not just the parts that photograph well. Finally, a story that sits at the intersection of
materials, science, and next generation electronics. A team led by Professor Boseo Kang at Sun
Qun Kuan University has solved a long standing puzzle about why polarity inversion occurs in only
some polymer semiconductors. The discovery published in advanced functional materials clarifies the
molecular mechanism behind this behavior that has puzzled researchers for years, understanding exactly
what controls polarity inversion gives material designers a much more precise lever. It should help
them create organic semiconductors with more predictable and stable performance, which is critical for
flexible electronics, wearable sensors, and printed circuits. These materials are attractive because
they can be solution processed at low temperatures, potentially making electronics cheaper and more
flexible than silicon allows. Every time we remove one of these, why does this only happen sometimes
mysteries? The path to reliable commercial devices gets a little clearer. Now here's something
most people get wrong about feeling sick. We tend to assume that loss of appetite during illness
is simply a side effect of general fatigue that your body is too busy fighting infection to bother
with food. In reality, specialized cells in your gut are actively detecting the presence of parasites
and bacteria. These cells then send targeted signals that specifically tell the brain to suppress hunger.
The process is surprisingly sophisticated. Right now, even as you listen, those gut cells are constantly
sampling what you've eaten and monitoring for microbial invaders. The signal builds gradually,
which is why you often feel fine at first, but then suddenly lose interest in food once the infection
takes hold. A single nerve pathway can relay these signals at speeds up to 120 meters per second.
It's an ancient defense mechanism designed to stop you from consuming more pathogens
while your immune system deals with the current threat. The next time you feel that sudden
disinterest in eating during an illness, it might help to remember it isn't weakness or random
fatigue. It's a deliberate protective response, paying attention to how quickly your appetite
returns after symptoms ease can even serve as a rough personal signal that your body has cleared
the immediate danger. Before we go, keep an eye on how those supercomputer simulations of molecular
machines develop in the coming months. There's real potential for them to start influencing drug
design in tangible ways. Next time, we'll also be watching for further updates on solar interior
discoveries and what they mean for space weather prediction. That's Planet Terri and Daily for
today. If you enjoyed the episode, a rating or review on Apple podcasts or Spotify really does help
new listeners find the show. I'm Patrick and Vancouver. Thanks for listening and I'll talk to you
tomorrow. This podcast is curated by Patrick, but generated using AI voice synthesis of my voice
using 11 labs. The primary reason to do this is I unfortunately don't have the time to be consistent
with generating all the content and wanted to focus on creating consistent and regular episodes
for all the themes that I enjoy and I hope others do as well.
